13 min read
“Motivating the committed outperforms persuading the
uncommitted.” — Seth Godin
This lesson is a customer-only addition to our World Building 101
for Marketing article (which we have included below for easy
reference). Everything in this lesson builds on that foundation.
§
World Building 101 for Marketing
Strategic Upstream Decisions:
World Building
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 22 --

Whatever You Think, Think the Opposite (p.90) by Paul Arden
Attracting and engaging with audiences are two critically
important skills for modern marketers, and there’s no shortage of
advice one-click away promising instant results.
“4 Steps to Building a Content-Generating Machine,” “Top 10 Best
Converting Headlines,” and “Top 5 Instagram In�uencer
Marketing Hacks” have appeared in our newsfeeds and inboxes
already this week.
Broadly speaking, these strategies fall in one of two camps.
On one side are the hardcore direct response marketers using ‘big
idea’-driven curiosity, bold promises, and a toolbox crammed full
of persuasion techniques to monetize attention as quickly as
possible.
On the other end of the spectrum are Gary Vaynerchuk-inspired
media producers who use social platforms, highly-optimized
content, and personality to build, entertain, and monetize
attention.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 22 --

Before we continue, let’s be very clear — both of these strategies
(and many in between) work. But, we think there’s a much better
way.
For the past 10+ years, we’ve been using and re�ning a completely
different approach to attract and engage with audiences that have
produced phenomenal results for us and our students.
That strategy is worldbuilding.
Instead of using aggressive direct response or media production
approach, we build “worlds” for our audiences to inhabit. Worlds
that acknowledge their implicit and explicit motivations, show
them better ways to solve their problems, meet their needs, ful�ll
their desires, and position ourselves as trusted, caring �duciaries.
Our approach is inspired by writers who build worlds that capture
their audiences’ attention, immerse their readers in a story that
feels real in every possible way, and pulls their readers forward
page by page, scene by scene with narrative tension.
These worlds can be familiar, like 1940s New York City for the
Corleone family, or they can include the magic of Harry Potter, the
Force in Star Wars, and the dragons, elves, hobbits, and dwarves of
Middle Earth.
When you build worlds, you’ll attract people who want to be
insiders — part of your tribe — and you’ll naturally repel everyone
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 22 --

else.
“People like us do things like this.” — Seth Godin
The question, of course, is how do we do it?
We’re glad you asked.
Let’s start with a big picture metaphor that can make this concept
more accessible.
Towns, Cities, and Worlds
Worldbuilding can feel overwhelming, especially at �rst. Start
small, and then expand from there.
Rather than try to build an entire world for your ideal prospects to
inhabit, think about creating a small “town.” That might be a
simple website and a Facebook page, for example.
You’ll attract a small audience, and that’s OK. As the audience
expands, your small town will gradually turn into a city as you
add more interconnected pages and other elements. That means
more content and more opportunities to engage with your
audience in meaningful ways. More engagement generates more
feedback too, and that creates a virtuous cycle.
The city you create will have a certain ‘feel’ that pulls your ideal
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 22 --

audience toward you. There are reasons why some people are
magnetically drawn to New Orleans vs. New York, Paris vs.
London, or Berlin vs. Dubai.
Just as New York attracts people who love the energy, buzz and
diverse population, it also repels people who don’t resonate with
the culture and nuances of the city.
The same is true for the world you’re building (and the world we
are continuously expanding here).
▪ Who do you want to attract?
▪ What’s important to them?
▪ How can you infuse your personality into your world in
ways that pull the right people toward you?
▪ Who are the people you don’t want as customers?
▪ How can you engineer insights and create Easter Eggs to be
discovered that builds a ‘sense of wonder’ within the world
you’re creating that pulls and tugs at them emotionally?
▪ In what ways can you engage meaningfully with your
audience (email, video, in-person, etc.)?
Once you understand the big picture metaphor of towns that
expand to cities and cities that grow into worlds, the next
distinction is the inner vs. outer world.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 22 --

The outer world is the content you create that is visible (and
discoverable) to everyone. Blog posts, manifestos, email
newsletters, pre-sells, social media, articles, free trainings,
speaking engagements, etc.
The “inner world” is accessible only to customers. Courses,
services, products, books, consulting, etc.
The inner and outer worlds are interconnected — all of the parts
interact within and across boundaries to produce emergent
effects and create superfans.
To help you think about some of the nuances of worldbuilding,
we’ve created a three-part framework inspired by an August 2018,
Ezra Klein interview with N.K. Jemisin — the �rst person to win
three Hugo Awards in a row for best novel and the �rst person to
win a Hugo Award for each of the three books in a trilogy.
“What makes Jemisin’s work so remarkable is the power and detail of
the worlds she builds for her characters, and her readers, to inhabit.” —
Ezra Klein
Jemisin teaches a worldbuilding seminar for science-�ction and
fantasy writers and she took Klein through a similar exercise
during the interview.
The depth of her insights and mastery of her craft are profound.
We may not need all of the worldbuilding tools in an award-
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 22 --

winning fantasy author’s toolbox, but we can use some of the
same ideas to inform and dramatically improve our marketing.
We’ve created a simple framework to follow that captures the
power of Jemisin’s ideas and brings them down to earth where
they’re more easily applied to our style of modern marketing.
Our framework for building worlds for our ideal prospects and
customers to inhabit includes three N.K. Jemisin-inspired
categories — science, politics, and culture.
Together, these categories point the way for us to develop
empathy for our prospects, clarity about the value we have to
share and how it relates to them, and integrity in our messaging.
Science represents the non-negotiable aspects of the world you’re
creating. These are your most deeply held values that you refuse
to violate. They are the equivalent of gravity in your world.
(In Beyond Entrepreneurship 2.0, author and consultant Jim
Collins refers to this as ‘Vision’ — a combination of core values,
beliefs, purpose, and mission.)
For example, four of our core values include:
▪ Relationships before transactions.
▪ Treat everyone like a customer regardless if money has
changed hands.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 22 --

▪ Always lead with meaningful value.
▪ Insights, not information.
These values are foundational for us. They are as much a part of
the world we create for our audience as the Laws of
Thermodynamics are necessary to understand physics.
Politics is about power — who has it in your market, and how is
that power exercised and displayed?
Is there an 800-pound gorilla that dominates your market (for
example, Amazon for e-commerce or The Motley Fool for personal
investing)?
Are there well-known personalities that set the tone (for example,
Tony Robbins in the personal development market)?
How do competitors sell to your audience?
Do they use hardcore, direct response-style persuasion with
‘boiler-room’ call centers, endlessly optimized funnels with every
automated marketing trick in the book, or in-person conferences
to build excitement and sell from the stage?
(Pro tip: it’s important to understand how your competitors are
marketing and selling, even if — especially if — you choose to
market and sell differently. One way to stand out in a market is to
draw attention to, and sell against, conventional strategies.)
www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 22 --

Culture identi�es what is most meaningful and important to
your audience as it relates to the value you’re able to create for
them.
▪ What signi�cant problems are they trying to solve?
▪ What desires would they most like to ful�ll?
▪ What matters most to them?
Think beyond functional needs and desires — how does your
audience want to feel as a result of their interactions with you and
the world you’ve created for them to inhabit? That’s where you
want to focus your attention.
Five-Step Framework Exercise
Here’s an exercise to turn these ideas into action that will bene�t
you (and your audience).
Step 1: Divide a document (or sheet of paper) into three columns
and title those columns Science, Politics, and Culture.
Step 2: Make a list of 3-5 values that guide your business
decision-making and put those in the Science column.
For example: “Always follow the Golden Rule.”
Step 3: Answer the following questions for the Politics column:
www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 22 --

▪ Who are the dominant players in your market?
▪ Who are the well-known personalities in your market?
▪ What are the most common ways that your audience is
marketed and sold to?
Step 4: Answer the following questions for your Culture column:
▪ What are the signi�cant problems you can help your
audience solve?
▪ What are the meaningful desires you can help your
audience achieve?
▪ What emotions are driving your audience’s decisions?
Step 5: Expand (and re�ne) these lists over time. Ask questions,
solicit feedback, and listen very carefully to what your audience
tells you.
Most of what you’ve articulated in this exercise will be for your
eyes only. It will inform your marketing, but it’s not your
marketing.
Like an iceberg, only 10% of your worldbuilding is visible to your
audience, according to Nora Jemisin. The rest is invisible to them
(but it’s there to keep pointing you in the right direction).
If you’re ready to put this into action, �nish the exercise above
and then use what you’ve discovered to create a landing page
www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 22 --

following our Landing Page Framework.
Final Thoughts
The most signi�cant difference between traditional marketing
methods and worldbuilding is the interconnectedness of the touch
points.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 11 of 22 --

A touch point is any marketing piece or other asset that your
audience can interact with (and �nd). It could be a Facebook ad,
landing page, blog post, video, article, email series, or an entire
course.
Worldbuilding creates interconnectedness and cohesion among
touch points which creates a sense of being a part of something
bigger in ways that are often surprising and genuinely relevant.
Worldbuilding underpins everything we do. Our ads, website, the
content we create (like this page you’re reading now), the emails
we write, and the courses we create — everything we do is part of
a larger system engineered for insights to �ourish.
We’re created a world for our ideal audience to explore at their
pace, not ours. No optimized funnels, no false scarcity, no
unnecessary urgency. For us — and many others — that has made
all of the difference.
§
In World Building 101, we identi�ed three categories that make up
the framework and texture of worldbuilding:
▪ Science (our values and vision),
▪ Politics (market power dynamics), and
▪ Culture (meaningful value creation).
www.getwsodo.com

-- 12 of 22 --

There is another characteristic of the marketing worlds we build
that we want to draw your attention to in this lesson.
That characteristic is Magic.
Magic is an intangible force like gravity — you can’t see it or touch
it — but it can be (emotionally) felt and channeled. When
channeled, the emotional response is ampli�ed (much like g-force
with gravity).
World Building: Magic
We know that a force exists in the people we seek to serve, and
when channeled in our direction, that force (expressed as
attraction and desire) is increased.
Worldbuilding is the mechanism for us to engineer these “force
amplifying” opportunities.
The more complex or valuable our solution to a problem is, the
more critical it is for us to build a world around those idea(s),
where other people can be attracted to, explore, and hang out.
A world so rich and captivating that the people we seek to serve
will want to spend disproportionate time in it, interact with our
content that resonates, and even share it with their social circles.
We create rabbit holes for our audience to �nd and explore. We
www.getwsodo.com

-- 13 of 22 --

strategically create interesting threads for them to pull.
There is an outer-world (our ads and website pages) and an inner-
world (emails, memberships, and hidden pages). Both are
important parts that interact with our audience in non-linear
ways to produce the emergent behavior of worldbuilding; and by
extension, the characteristic of magic can be observed.
One of the implications of worldbuilding, is marketing that pulls
and tugs at people with the invisible force we’ve already
explained.
Worldbuilding is not a linear �ow like a sale funnel, but it can
consist of linear elements within a larger structure of
interconnected pages and emails and components.
Here’s a visualization of a small world (which will expand over
time).
www.getwsodo.com

-- 14 of 22 --

Here’s a much larger world (representing both the inner and outer
world).
www.getwsodo.com

-- 15 of 22 --

Because this is not a linear experience, every person who enters
our world, and interacts with it, will have a uniquely different
experience. Each touchpoint channels their inner force towards
us, bit by bit, page by page, email by email, amplifying over time.
There will be a moment — that’s different for everyone — where
they decide, then and there, that they will be a customer. Money
may change hands days, weeks, or even months later, but it’ll
happen with absolute certainty.
The implication, the experience of feeling worldbuilding in action,
has that affect on people. It draws them in, and it does it in a way
that’s meaningfully different for each person.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 16 of 22 --

What does effective worldbuilding look like?
Our site is our expression. But there are many other examples,
and what’s noticeable is none look the same. They’re all uniquely
different in meaningful ways.
There are no templates to follow, only examples to be inspired by.
Some worlds may seem like a blog, but aren’t when you look
closer, when you venture down the rabbit holes, when you’re one
of the insiders — “People like us do things like this”.
To help demonstrate this idea, we’re going to apply a “version
number” to you as you read this. To keep things simple, let’s say
that right now, in this moment, you are Version 1 . The actual
number is unimportant.
Work with us here, it’ll be worth it. Promise.
Version 1 represents your current reality (paradigm) and
worldview. Your beliefs that inform your decisions, actions,
behaviors, and how you see and understand the world and your
place in it. For this course, that can be how you currently think
about email marketing, its role as a channel to reach an audience,
which both inform your (current) behavior and actions.
We’ll start by getting a little philosophical; there is an “invisible
force” within you that seeks improvement, change — a desire or
existential need to “level up” your current operating system.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 17 of 22 --

The Internal Force
It could be the desire to to learn email marketing (which will
bene�t you �nancially), or a more abstract skill acquisition like
the craft of creative writing.
This invisible force pulls and tugs at you to improve. Sometimes it
manifests as a little voice in your head that whispers in your ear
(You can do this…), other times it’s loud and obnoxious. (Dude, get
your shit together!)
If you’ve ever played the game Super Mario, you’ll know that
Mario seeks mushrooms and stars and �owers to transform into
Super Mario on his adventures. Super Mario is stronger, faster,
more badass.
Using Super Mario as a metaphor, we can visualize this idea like
this:
You work to level-up bit by bit, small win by small win, acquiring
new skills and level-ups until, when you look back in time, the
www.getwsodo.com

-- 18 of 22 --

transformation is noticeable and measurable.
Think about that dynamic.
You probably don’t wake up each morning wondering where you
will spend your hard-earned dollars and “attention credits.”
Most days your skill acquisition is new information from
interacting with articles, your social news feeds, and watching
videos. Stuff that requires time and attention credits, an
opportunity cost.
You’re not opposed to spending money when it’ll serve your needs
(faster, more direct, in a better way, etc.).
This is your current reality.
It’s also the reality of millions of prospects each day, all working
towards a version of themselves that’s better in some meaningful
way.
The invisible force that pulls and tugs at people to improve needs
to already exist in the people you seek to serve, pulling and
tugging at them to seek improvement.
People who are already internally motivated to improve.
Our job as modern marketers is not to persuade the uncommitted
— that’s a fool’s errand — but to channel the force within the
www.getwsodo.com

-- 19 of 22 --

committed, increasing its emotional pull in our direction.
Doing this well is magical.
When we internalize that this powerful force already exists in our
prospects, we realize that our job as a modern marketer is not to
exert an opposing force in an effort to sell-fast.
The big insight is that we can provide the “leveling up”
EXPERIENCE when we present information as insights for our
prospects to �nd and bene�t from.
No catches.
Much like we hope this lesson is having on you…
You’re very likely slightly better off from this interaction, this
short experience.
Which means you’re probably not Version 1 anymore.
You’re Version 2 . Or Version 1.3 .
Something more, not less. We’ve given, not taken, and that’s the
frame we want you to use when thinking about building worlds
for your audience.
Every email you create in AoE is an opportunity to give
something. Here’s the visualization that better represents a better
www.getwsodo.com

-- 20 of 22 --

prospect.
Each insight is a little win for you. Something that incrementally
improves your operating system version by 0.1 each time.
To be clear, an insight is not just helpful information. Helpful
information is a dime a dozen. Helpful information has a low
barrier to entry (Google search) and offers little value other than
being helpful within a certain context.
An insight is altogether different.
An insight has a payload that delivers power and has impact
behind it. It can rock worlds and shift realities. The feeling is
unmistakable.
Useful information is a paper airplane. A powerful insight is a
freaking rocket ship! �	�
Like the German fairy tale, Hansel and Gretel, insights are like
breadcrumbs for the best prospects. They lead them along a path,
www.getwsodo.com

-- 21 of 22 --

all while upgrading a prospect’s worldview in a way that is
fundamentally valuable to them.
Welcome to a new paradigm, a different way of building
customer attraction, experiences that are meaningful and
powerful.
Please post your comments and questions below.
—André & Shawn
www.getwsodo.com

-- 22 of 22 --
